scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: Meets most pass criteria (7 rules extracted, demand identified as undefined, confidence at 30, no values invented, 4+ escalations with clarifying questions, 8 edge cases surfacing gaps) but violates the 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' pass requirement by producing 6 acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-6). The rubric explicitly lists writing Given/When/Then criteria as a failure mode. The criteria use parameterized placeholders rather than invented values, which mitigates severity, placing this in strong partial territory rather than fail.

observations.text: Extracted 7 business rules (BR-1 through BR-7), exceeding the 5-rule minimum, each with invariant classification and detailed justification
Confidence level of 30 is well within the pass threshold of <= 40
Demand explicitly identified as undefined in escalations, low_confidence_areas, and given its own dedicated escalation entry
No specific values invented — all thresholds and bounds are parameterized ('configured high-demand threshold', 'configured maximum price guardrail')
4 detailed escalations covering guardrails ambiguity, demand definition, real-time definition, and competitive-vs-profitable tension, plus 8 edge cases that surface additional critical gaps
Correctly flagged the CEO's 'probably' regarding guardrails as alarming and recommended treating guardrails as non-negotiable
11 low-confidence areas enumerated, demonstrating thorough ambiguity recognition
Wrote 6 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-6) despite the rubric explicitly requiring none be written due to input vagueness

The acceptance criteria section contradicts the agent's own confidence assessment — at 30% confidence with massive ambiguity, writing testable criteria sends a mixed signal about specification readiness

The system adjusts delivery prices based on demand levels, aiming to balance competitiveness with profitability, and will operate in a fully automated manner with some form of price guardrails.","bounded_context":"Pricing / Rate Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definitio

trials.justification: Meets most pass criteria (8 rules extracted with invariant/confidence analysis, 5+ clarifying questions via escalations, 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined in escalations and low_confidence_areas, confidence.level=35 which is <=40, no specific values invented). However, critically violates the pass requirement 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' — AC-1 through AC-6 are explicit Given/When/Then acceptance criteria. The rubric lists 'Writing Given/When/Then acceptance criteria from this vague input' as a failure mode. Six criteria without heavy caveats pushes this beyond 'few minimal criteria with heavy caveats' (partial threshold). Confidence level of 35 is below the partial range of 41-60, which is actually better than partial expects. Net: strong analysis undermined by premature criteria authoring.

observations.text: Extracted 8 business rules (BR-1 through BR-8), exceeding the 5-rule minimum, with invariant classifications and justifications
Confidence.level=35, appropriately low for a vague CEO pitch — meets <=40 threshold
'Demand' explicitly called out as undefined in escalation #1, low_confidence_areas, and ubiquitous_language definition
5 escalations surface critical questions: demand definition, guardrail specifics, real-time definition, competitive vs profitable priority, guardrail optionality
No specific numeric values invented — all thresholds reference 'configured' or 'defined' parameters
Excellent edge cases including regulatory price gouging, price whiplash, and guardrail misconfiguration
Correctly identified 'probably' qualifier on guardrails as a confidence-reducing signal
Wrote 6 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-6) despite input being too vague — this is an explicit failure mode in the rubric
Criteria are not heavily caveated — they read as actionable specs rather than tentative hypotheses
Business rules include testable_assertion fields that are essentially mini-acceptance criteria, further contradicting the 'too vague for criteria' stance

trials.justification: Meets most pass criteria (6 rules extracted, 11+ clarifying questions in low_confidence_areas, 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined, confidence.level=30, no specific values invented) but fails the critical 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' requirement. AC-1 through AC-5 are full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, which is listed as a failure mode. The criteria use placeholder terms rather than invented values, placing this in partial territory rather than fail.

observations.text: 6 business rules extracted (BR-1 through BR-6), exceeding the 5-rule minimum
11 items in low_confidence_areas effectively serve as clarifying questions, plus 3 detailed escalations with structured options
Demand explicitly called out as undefined in multiple places: low_confidence_areas, escalation #1
confidence.level=30, well within the <=40 pass threshold
No specific values invented — all thresholds and bounds left as unknowns
Excellent escalation quality: first escalation explicitly recommends 'Do not begin implementation' and flags every ambiguity as a potential production incident
7 edge cases identified, several quite sophisticated (price flickering/hysteresis, quote-vs-confirmation race)
Wrote 5 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5) despite input being too vague — this directly violates the pass requirement 'No Given/When/Then criteria written'
Business rules lack individual confidence ratings per rule — only invariant flags are provided, not per-rule confidence levels
The acceptance criteria give an impression of implementability that contradicts the escalation's own recommendation to not begin implementation
